title: Paper Explained: VFIG: Vectorizing Complex Figures in SVG with Vision-Language Models - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle: AI Turns Complex Figures into Scalable Vector Graphics

content.background:
Before this work, turning a lost vector figure back into a editable SVG was a big pain point in science and design. Often the original vector file is gone, and all that remains is a flat image (like a photo of a diagram). Rebuilding the vector version by hand is slow and labor-intensive, and it requires people with specialized vector-drawing skills. Auto-tracing tools exist, but they usually produce rough lines, misplaced elements, or lose important details like labels and precise relationships between parts. In fields that rely on precise diagrams—think graphs, schematics, or layered diagrams—the slightest misalignment can mislead readers, so people are discouraged from reusing or adapting figures.

A second big challenge is that there wasn’t enough data to teach a computer how to do this well. Researchers needed examples that look and behave like real, professional diagrams, not simple or toy figures. Most existing datasets were small, simplistic, or didn’t capture the complex layouts and hierarchies found in actual scientific figures. Without lots of realistic examples, it’s hard for a model to learn how different parts relate to each other, where elements should be placed, and how to preserve the overall structure when converting from a raster image to a vector one. This also made it hard to judge progress, because the usual evaluation methods didn’t fully capture whether the reconstructed figure would be usable for editing or reuse.

All of this meant a real bottleneck: even with clues from the image, automated reconstruction of high-quality, edit-friendly vector figures was unreliable, slow, and not scalable. The motivation for this line of work is to reduce the manual burden, improve reproducibility, and enable easy reuse of figures across papers and designs. To move the field forward, researchers needed large, realistic data and better ways to measure success—beyond pretty visuals—to judge whether the reconstructed vector truly preserves the figure’s structure, layout, and meaning.
content.methodology:
Here’s a beginner-friendly breakdown of what the paper does and why it’s interesting.

- What problem they’re solving
  - Many technical figures in papers exist only as flat images (raster like PNG/JPEG). If you want to edit or scale them, you’d love the original vector file (SVG), but that source is often lost. VFIG aims to automatically convert these raster figures into editable SVGs, using vision-language models that can “read” an image and “write” a vector description.

- The big idea in simple terms
  - Think of SVGs as a recipe made of building blocks: lines, curves, rectangles, circles, text, colors, and how those blocks are organized into layers and groups. VFIG teaches a model to look at a figure and output a correct set of those blocks that, when assembled, recreate the original image in vector form. The goal isn’t just to copy pixels but to capture the underlying structure so the result is scalable and editable.

- How they built the data and the model
  - They created VFIG-DATA, a large collection (66,000 pairs) of figures paired with their corresponding SVGs. It includes real diagrams from papers and procedurally generated ones, so the model sees lots of shapes, layouts, and styles.
  - The model treats SVGs as a composition of recurring primitives and hierarchical structures (think lines, arrows, boxes, groups, and how those pieces nest). This helps the model learn not just individual shapes but also how they fit together to form a coherent figure.

- The training approach (coarse-to-fine, like learning to build with blocks)
  - Step 1: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT). The model learns basic, atomic primitives first—the individual building blocks like a line, a rectangle, a curved path, or text—so it can reliably create simple pieces.
  - Step 2: Reinforcement learning (RL) refinement. The model then practices composing those primitives into complete figures, paying attention to the overall layout, consistency, and tricky edge cases where parts connect or overlap. RL gives the model flexible feedback to improve how well the whole SVG matches the target figure, not just the small pieces in isolation.
  - Conceptually, it’s a two-stage curriculum: first master the bricks, then learn to assemble them into a faithful, well-organized diagram.

- How they measure success
  - They also built VFIG-BENCH, a testing suite with new metrics focused on the structural integrity of complex figures—how well the vectorized result preserves the figure’s intended shapes, relationships, and layout.
  - In results, VFIG achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models and performs on par with a strong private model (GPT-5.2) on this task, according to the VLM-Judge metric they use.

In short, VFIG makes it possible to turn difficult, editable vector graphics back from flat figure images by training a vision-language model to learn both the basic drawing blocks and how to arrange them into coherent, structurally faithful SVGs. It’s like teaching a designer to read a diagram and recreate a clean Lego-assembly version that you can edit later, without needing the original vector file.
content.results:
VFIG delivers a practical way to turn flat, raster images of scientific and technical figures back into editable SVG files. The authors created a large, diverse dataset (66,000 figure–SVG pairs) drawn from real-world papers and procedurally generated diagrams, which gives the model many examples of how complex figures are built. They then train the model in two stages: first, it learns the basic building blocks (the little vector pieces like lines, curves, and simple shapes); then it uses a refinement stage (reinforcement learning) to make sure the entire diagram looks coherent, keeps the right layout, and handles tricky edge cases. They also built VFIG-BENCH, a dedicated test suite with new ways to judge whether the produced SVG truly preserves the figure’s structure, not just how it looks at the pixel level.

Compared to earlier work, this is a big step forward. Previously, turning a raster image back into a high-quality vector diagram was hard, often manual, and relied on small datasets that didn’t capture the complexity of professional figures. VFIG combines two key ideas that matter in practice: a large, varied dataset that helps the model learn real-world figure patterns, and a clever training plan that first locks onto simple building blocks and later fine-tunes the whole diagram for global accuracy. On open-source models, VFIG achieves the best performance so far, and it reaches a level of quality close to one of the top private systems, which is notable for a research-focused effort.

The practical impact is substantial. This work can save researchers, students, and designers a lot of time by letting them recover editable vector versions of figures from published images, enabling easy edits, scaling, and reuse without recreating diagrams from scratch. It also helps with reproducibility and accessibility—figures can be edited or repurposed without losing precision or structure. Beyond SVGs, the ideas—large, varied data; a coarse-to-fine learning path; and targeted structural evaluation—could influence how we tackle other complex visual-to-vector or diagram-understanding tasks in the future.
content.significance:
This paper matters today because it tackles a stubborn bottleneck in science and design: when original vector source files are lost, researchers are stuck with flat images that are hard to edit, scale, or remix. VFIG shows that a vision-language model can reliably turn complex raster figures back into editable SVGs, not just roughly, but with high fidelity to the original layout and topology. The authors back this up with a large, public dataset (VFIG-DATA, 66K figure-SVG pairs) and a coarse-to-fine training recipe that moves from learning atomic SVG primitives to refining global diagram structure through reinforcement learning. The result is a practical path to making scientific graphics more reproducible, accessible (SVGs are screen-reader friendly and easily scalable), and reusable across papers and workflows. The reported performance—state-of-the-art among open-source models and competitive with GPT-5.2 on their VFIG-BENCH—illustrates that this is not just a niche capability but a scalable technology with real-world impact.

In terms of long-term significance, VFIG helped push forward the idea that multimodal models should produce structured, editable outputs, not just pixels or natural language. The coarse-to-fine curriculum and the emphasis on hierarchical SVG primitives (and the accompanying structural benchmarks) influenced later research into structured-output generation for diagrams, circuits, chemistry drawings, and other technical illustrations. By showing how to align raster-to-vector tasks with robust evaluation metrics, the work provided a blueprint for building AI systems that respect geometry and topology, which is crucial for any tool meant to assist professionals in design, engineering, and academia.

Specific applications and connections to modern AI systems are becoming evident. Tools used in publishing workflows—LaTeX editors, Overleaf-like platforms, or diagram editors integrated into word processors—could incorporate raster-to-vector conversion to automatically recover editable figures from published PDFs or low-resolution images. Accessibility tooling benefits as well, since semantic SVGs can be interpreted by screen readers and reflowed for different devices. For students and everyday users, this parallels how ChatGPT-like systems with image understanding could take a photo of a diagram and return an editable SVG or even generate a corrected version from a natural-language description. In short, VFIG helps bridge the gap between understanding visual figures and producing high-quality, reusable vector graphics, a capability that will become increasingly central as AI assistants take on more design and documentation roles.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Coarse-to-Fine Training: The Heart of VFIG
concept_explanation.content:
Think of turning a complex diagram from a photo into a clean, editable SVG like building a LEGO model from a blurred image. First you grab the rough structure: where the main shapes sit, how many boxes there are, where the arrows point. That’s the coarse stage. Then you start filling in the details: the exact shapes, sizes, colors, and text inside each box. Finally you fine-tune how everything fits together so the whole diagram looks coherent and true to the original. This is the intuition behind coarse-to-fine training in VFIG.

In VFIG, the model is trained in two main stages that mirror that idea. The first stage is supervised fine-tuning (SFT), where the model learns the atomic primitives that make up SVG figures—think basic building blocks like lines, rectangles, circles, curves, text blocks, and their attributes (position, size, color). The model also learns how these primitives typically combine to form meaningful diagram pieces. This stage focuses on getting the local, small-scale details right: correctly predicting a single line’s endpoints, a label’s font size, or a color fill for a shape. The second stage is reinforcement learning (RL) refinement, which shifts the focus from just individual primitives to the entire diagram. Here the model is rewarded for global fidelity: how well the overall layout matches the ground-truth SVG, how consistent the placement and alignment are across many primitives, and how it handles tricky topological situations like overlapping shapes, curved connectors, or edge-case layering. The RL stage nudges the model to produce diagrams that aren’t just piecewise accurate but also coherent as a single, well-formed figure.

To make this work, the authors leverage a large dataset called VFIG-DATA with 66,000 figure-SVG pairs. The coarse stage teaches the model the vocabulary of primitives and how they typically appear in real figures, giving it a solid foundation for building rough, correct diagrams from raster inputs. The RL stage then takes over to optimize for the whole picture: ensuring that related components line up (layout consistency), that the connections between parts are topologically correct (no broken arrows or misplaced anchors), and that the final SVG is faithful to the reference when scaled or edited. This two-step curriculum helps the model avoid the common pitfall of trying to predict a perfect global structure all at once, which can be unstable given the many possible correct ways to compose a complex figure.

Why is this coarse-to-fine approach important? Many scientific and technical figures exist only as raster images or are redrawn from scratch, making them hard to edit, scale, or reuse. By first learning reliable atomic primitives and then refining for global coherence, VFIG can produce high-fidelity, editable SVGs that preserve both local details and the overall diagram layout. Practical applications are wide: researchers can convert old papers’ figures into editable SVGs for quick updates, design systems can reuse standardized diagram components, accessibility tools can better render figures for screen readers, and automated pipelines can generate editable diagrams for simulations or tutorials. In short, coarse-to-fine training provides a principled pathway from rough, rough-draft reconstructions to polished, publication-ready vector diagrams.

summary: This paper introduces VFIG, a family of vision-language models that convert complex raster figures into high-fidelity SVGs, backed by a large 66K figure-to-SVG dataset, a coarse-to-fine training curriculum (SFT followed by RL), and the VFIG-BENCH evaluation suite, achieving state-of-the-art results among open models and parity with GPT-5.2.
